title: How close is Iran to having a nuclear weapon? | The Economist

transcript.text: So there are a lot of reasons to worry
about events in Iran right now, but I
guess top of mind for many is 440 kilos
of highlyenriched uranium that your
agency estimated was there. Do you know
where that uranium is now? Well, we were
inspecting that material as part of our
regular
um program of inspections in Iran. And
we were actually inspecting up to just a
few days before the 12 day war. So uh
our estimations were quite accurate to
the gram. Uh we don't know now and this
is why we need to go back. Uh I think uh
the the overall
evaluation assessment is that this
material is where it was um at at that
time and there hasn't been a a move of
it.
>> So should we all calm down because we we
have had reassuring messages. So,
President Trump says that there are
satellites watching it. It's deeply
buried uh nuclear dust, I believe, is
the the phrase he uses and it's under
very exacting surveillance. So, we
should all calm down. Are you calm about
not knowing exactly where it is right
now?
>> I'm neither calm nor anxious. I think
this is uh material at almost weapon
grade and it has to be checked. uh Iran
until today is a member of the nuclear
non-prololiferation treaty. It has to
account for all its nuclear material and
facilities and it hasn't been doing
that. There are logical reasons for
that. There was the 12-day war. After
that war, we were trying to negotiate
our way back to Iran. And of course,
this new episode unsuccessfully, I
should say, and this new uh
military episode of war uh has prevented
us from doing so. But that doesn't mean
that we should not go uh back uh and
inspect this material because this
material if diverted if we if we lose u
knowledge of it or or its whereabouts uh
it could end up uh in a in a program
which is not peaceful. So u as I say we
have a a duty to account for it and of
course when conditions allow Iran should
be facilitating the access of the IEA
inspectors. The IAEA is the nuclear
referee. A central claim going into this
latest war in Iran from America and
Israel was that there was an imminent
chance that Iran was on the point of
having a nuclear weapon. Is that right?
Well, again, uh when it comes to
evaluations or assessments,
uh this is on the eye of the beholder,
as we would say, uh countries and in
this case the United States or or Israel
may have a an a judgment on where this
is going.
And this I cannot dispute that from from
from our side we we said two things and
it's important because sometimes people
have a tendency to focus on one part of
the comment and not the other. We we
said and even in my reports reports to
the board of governors of the IEA and
things like this we've been saying that
whilst there was not no systematic
organized uh um institutionalized
program that we could uh see uh for for
the production of of nuclear weapons. At
the same time you have all this material
we had uh limited access uh to other
places where we had found traces of
uranium. I mean the the the mosaic was
quite complicated. So we were saying and
we were insisting uh on Iran that they
um in order to clarify to dispel any
doubts that they should cooperate with
us that did not happen or at least in
the way it should uh have uh happened.
So clearly uh some concerning elements
there and then of course the judgment of
of countries is for for them to judge
and to see whether it was accurate or
not.
>> Do you worry that this regime now has a
huge incentive to get a bomb to go over
the threshold because being at the
threshold did not save them.
>> But I think it's a contrary.
I think when you see what has been
happening
uh I believe that u uh if anything this
uh should be an incentive to cooperate.
They continue to argue and I've had
long conversations with the foreign
minister and others. They continue to
argue that uh it is not in their
intentions, it is not in their plans,
etc., etc. But one thing still is
missing. Uh in this uh area promises are
not enough.
Statements are not enough. Even with due
respect, fatwas are not enough. You have
to be uh able to show what you have and
what you are developing. And when you
don't do that, then of course you enter
into an area of uncertainty. I was just
talking to an ambassador who said, "Look
at the Middle East. Look at the leaders
who negotiated with the West on their
nuclear programs, Saddam Hussein,
Muhammad Gaddafi, and Ayatah Ali Kami.
They're all now dead."
>> Who's still alive? The North Korean
dictator, Kim Jong-un, because he got a
nuclear bomb. So, isn't the lesson for
every death spot on Earth? Get a bomb
and you won't be attacked.
>> Well, you could argue that those who die
died in the effort maybe of getting
there. Uh so u yeah the Kim Jong-un's u
u example uh should be uh or the North
Korean example should be an incentive
for all to go back to the negotiating
table.
>> Why isn't it an incentive to get a bomb?
It's not that I would like to see all
these countries get bombs but really
genuinely the countries that survive are
the ones that get a bomb. We even saw
the Russian foreign minister in
Pyongyang last summer saying he totally
understands the timely decision by North
Korea to get a bomb and that is why
they're not being attacked. I mean
that's it's horrible but it's true,
isn't it?
>> Get a bomb and you're safe.
>> Uh I don't believe that.
>> Why not?
>> Because I think that uh uh the uh
argument that nuclear weapons are going
to be getting you uh safety and and
protection is not valid. the case of uh
the
>> it's valid for North Korea, isn't it?
>> It's valid for North Korea which is in a
geographical political
environment which is completely
different from the Middle East.
>> Is there a way out of this Iran crisis?
I mean, if you're if you're a hawk in
Washington, you say we're going to have
to bomb them. We're going to have to
bomb this if needs to be this program
out of existence. Can you as a technical
matter? Yeah. As the nuclear policeman,
>> can a program like Iran's be bombed out
of existence?
Not completely. No. No. And this is
something that uh that uh uh it's
obvious because you cannot unlearn what
you've learned.
>> But if I was a hawk, I'd say so then
kill all the scientists with this
knowledge in their head. Bomb all of the
sites. There will be people saying that
in maybe Israel or in America.
>> Well, that would be unique in in
contemporary history. This idea that you
can destroy completely a country. uh I I
I don't think that that is realistic as
an option honestly apart from moral
considerations and ethical
considerations which of course uh would
would in my in my personal view uh deem
it unacceptable and and maybe horrific
uh but um I I don't think a country in
with the dimension and with the economic
maturity of Iran can simply be uh you
know um prevented
from from from doing this. And mind you,
this for example, the area of enrichment
has its complexities, but it's not
something that it is, you know, of a
level of sophistication that and they
they uh reached that if you compare
between the JCPOA
>> time,
>> the old Obama era nuclear agreement.
>> Exactly.
>> Yeah. that was um looking at a very
simple type of centrifuge machine there.
Well, in a in in the space of three to
four years since the JCPOA was
abandoned, well, Iran very quickly
developed uh ultra centrifuges of you
know of of much uh better performance
and and and
accuracy
and output. So uh and that is there and
that is something that can be uh done in
in in in workshops in many cities in so
how you cannot stop that militarily.
>> So then if you can't bomb it completely
out of existence if you can't kill
everyone who has the knowledge in their
heads what is the way out of this what
is there a way of stopping this nuclear
program? Is there a way of stopping Iran
getting a nuclear bomb?
>> What you need to stop is a nuclear
weapons program. a nuclear program any
country.
>> So, how do you stop a nuclear weapons
program?
>> Well, by having an inspection regime, by
allowing the IEA to do its job.
>> Are we closer or further from that since
this latest war? Has that latest war
helped you or hurt your job?
>> Well, I think it makes it more complex.
